articles.title: Book smarts and life smarts are driven by the exact same intelligence, study finds
articles.summary: A new study reveals that everyday knowledge gained through random life experiences and facts learned in a classroom are driven by the exact same underlying mental ability, challenging how psychologists measure human intelligence.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals that everyday knowledge gained through random life experiences and facts learned in a classroom are driven by the exact same underlying mental ability, challenging how psychologists measure human intelligence.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study reveals that everyday knowledge gained through random life experiences and facts learned in a classroom are driven by the exact same underlying mental ability, challenging how psychologists measure human intelligence.

articles.title: Yale scientists may have found how Parkinson's disease spreads through the brain
articles.summary: Yale scientists discovered two neuron surface proteins that appear to help spread the toxic protein linked to Parkinson’s disease. Blocking these proteins in mice dramatically reduced disease progression, offering a potential new target for future therapies.
articles.aiSummary: Yale scientists discovered two neuron surface proteins that appear to help spread the toxic protein linked to Parkinson’s disease. Blocking these proteins in mice dramatically reduced disease progression, offering a potential new target for future therapies.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Yale scientists discovered two neuron surface proteins that appear to help spread the toxic protein linked to Parkinson’s disease. Blocking these proteins in mice dramatically reduced disease progression, offering a potential new target for future therapies.

articles.title: Coffee consumption linked to slower cellular aging in people with severe mental illness
articles.summary: Patients with severe mental health conditions who drink three to four cups of coffee daily may possess cells that appear roughly five years younger. A new study links moderate coffee intake to longer telomeres, the protective caps on human chromosomes.
articles.aiSummary: Patients with severe mental health conditions who drink three to four cups of coffee daily may possess cells that appear roughly five years younger. A new study links moderate coffee intake to longer telomeres, the protective caps on human chromosomes.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Patients with severe mental health conditions who drink three to four cups of coffee daily may possess cells that appear roughly five years younger. A new study links moderate coffee intake to longer telomeres, the protective caps on human chromosomes.

articles.title: Context Graphs for Proactive Enterprise Agents
articles.summary: arXiv:2607.07721v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and agentic frameworks have advanced enterprise AI considerably, yet agents remain fundamentally reactive: they wait for a human query before acting. This paper argues that genuine enterprise productivity gains require proactive agents: systems that surface relevant, actionable information to workers before they ask. We propose the Context Graph, a live relational data structure that models enterprise entities, their relationships, and state transitions over time. Built on this graph, we define a Delta Detecti

articles.title: AI-integrated models for assessing agricultural resilience
articles.summary: arXiv:2607.07759v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Agricultural supply chains are vulnerable to disruptions through linked biophysical and economic systems. We develop an AI-powered tool that integrates economic models (GTAP) with biophysical models (APSIM) to analyze supply chain shocks, enabling policymakers and market participants to assess cross-disciplinary impacts through queries and responses written in natural language.

articles.title: Adversarial Social Epistemology for Assemblies of Humans and Large Language Models
articles.summary: arXiv:2607.07760v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: We outline an adversarial social epistemology (ASE) for densely interactive communicative landscapes in which public assertions are scaffolded by chains of testimony, inference, institutional certification, and tacit trust. In such landscapes, agents have incentives and affordances to distort, color, omit, fabricate, or strategically under-specify information for private, reputational, rhetorical, or material gains. We argue that these phenomena are not adequately captured by familiar descriptions of epistemic bubbles, echo chambers, or misinform

articles.title: A Mediterranean diet is linked to better psychological well-being in older adults
articles.summary: Older adults who follow a Mediterranean diet experience higher levels of positive psychological well-being. A new study suggests this eating pattern even provided an emotional buffer against the stress of the early COVID-19 pandemic.
articles.aiSummary: Older adults who follow a Mediterranean diet experience higher levels of positive psychological well-being. A new study suggests this eating pattern even provided an emotional buffer against the stress of the early COVID-19 pandemic.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Older adults who follow a Mediterranean diet experience higher levels of positive psychological well-being. A new study suggests this eating pattern even provided an emotional buffer against the stress of the early COVID-19 pandemic.

articles.title: Aligning Clinical Needs and AI Capabilities: A Survey on LLMs for Medical Reasoning
articles.summary: arXiv:2607.07761v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as important tools in healthcare, showing growing potential for clinical reasoning and patient care. This survey examines recent progress in medical LLMs, focusing on reasoning applications and requirements. We present a dual-view approach that connects clinical practice with computational methods. On the clinical side, we establish a five-level competency scheme following Miller's Pyramid, progressing from knowledge recall to dynamic case management. articles.title: Cannabis use in older adults not linked to faster cognitive decline or dementia
articles.summary: As cannabis use grows among older adults, so do concerns about memory loss. However, a new study found that prior or occasional cannabis use is not associated with faster cognitive decline or a higher risk of dementia.
articles.aiSummary: As cannabis use grows among older adults, so do concerns about memory loss. However, a new study found that prior or occasional cannabis use is not associated with faster cognitive decline or a higher risk of dementia.
articles.aiSummaryEn: As cannabis use grows among older adults, so do concerns about memory loss. However, a new study found that prior or occasional cannabis use is not associated with faster cognitive decline or a higher risk of dementia.

articles.title: Study finds #SexPositivity posts on Instagram often reinforce women’s objectification and narrow beauty ideals
articles.summary: Instagram posts tagged with #SexPositivity often promote objectification, narrow beauty standards, and commercial branding more than sexual empowerment, according to research published in Sex Roles.
articles.aiSummary: Instagram posts tagged with #SexPositivity often promote objectification, narrow beauty standards, and commercial branding more than sexual empowerment, according to research published in Sex Roles.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Instagram posts tagged with #SexPositivity often promote objectification, narrow beauty standards, and commercial branding more than sexual empowerment, according to research published in Sex Roles.

articles.title: New research offers evidence of a long-term connection between pornography use and depression
articles.summary: A new longitudinal study reveals that adults who frequently view pornography tend to experience more depressive symptoms. This consistent link remains stable over time and exists independently of a person's age, gender, or personal moral beliefs.
articles.aiSummary: A new longitudinal study reveals that adults who frequently view pornography tend to experience more depressive symptoms. This consistent link remains stable over time and exists independently of a person's age, gender, or personal moral beliefs.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new longitudinal study reveals that adults who frequently view pornography tend to experience more depressive symptoms. This consistent link remains stable over time and exists independently of a person's age, gender, or personal moral beliefs.

articles.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
articles.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.
articles.aiSummary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. articles.title: Childhood trauma linked to depression in fathers of toddlers via masculine role stress
articles.summary: A new study reveals how a history of childhood trauma can lead to depressive symptoms in fathers raising toddlers, operating through a sequential pathway of masculine gender role stress and emotional dysregulation.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals how a history of childhood trauma can lead to depressive symptoms in fathers raising toddlers, operating through a sequential pathway of masculine gender role stress and emotional dysregulation.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study reveals how a history of childhood trauma can lead to depressive symptoms in fathers raising toddlers, operating through a sequential pathway of masculine gender role stress and emotional dysregulation.

articles.title: Harvard scientists tracked 200,000 people to see how one common form of distress alters the future
articles.summary: New research spanning 23 countries shows that everyday suffering takes a lingering toll on human flourishing. Scientists found that adults experiencing distress reported lower life satisfaction, poorer health, and more loneliness up to a year later.
articles.aiSummary: New research spanning 23 countries shows that everyday suffering takes a lingering toll on human flourishing. Scientists found that adults experiencing distress reported lower life satisfaction, poorer health, and more loneliness up to a year later.
articles.aiSummaryEn: New research spanning 23 countries shows that everyday suffering takes a lingering toll on human flourishing. Scientists found that adults experiencing distress reported lower life satisfaction, poorer health, and more loneliness up to a year later.

articles.title: Second pregnancy changes the brain in surprising new ways
articles.summary: Researchers found that every pregnancy rewires the brain in its own way, with a second pregnancy bringing a different pattern of changes than the first. The discoveries could lead to better ways to recognize and treat maternal mental health challenges, including peripartum depression.
articles.aiSummary: Researchers found that every pregnancy rewires the brain in its own way, with a second pregnancy bringing a different pattern of changes than the first. The discoveries could lead to better ways to recognize and treat maternal mental health challenges, including peripartum depression.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Researchers found that every pregnancy rewires the brain in its own way, with a second pregnancy bringing a different pattern of changes than the first. The discoveries could lead to better ways to recognize and treat maternal mental health challenges, including peripartum depression.

articles.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
articles.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

articles.title: New MRI breakthrough reveals the brain and eye like never before
articles.summary: Scientists have redesigned a key piece of MRI hardware using metamaterials, allowing existing scanners to produce clearer images of difficult-to-see parts of the body in less time. The breakthrough could improve diagnoses, make scans more comfortable, and open the door to new medical imaging and treatment applications.
articles.aiSummary: Scientists have redesigned a key piece of MRI hardware using metamaterials, allowing existing scanners to produce clearer images of difficult-to-see parts of the body in less time. articles.title: Antidepressants may normalize brain tissue changes caused by chronic depression
articles.summary: A new study reveals how antidepressant medications physically change the brain. Researchers found that treating chronic depression normalizes abnormal brain tissue microstructure, suggesting the initial abnormalities were actually the brain’s attempt to cope with severe symptoms.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals how antidepressant medications physically change the brain. Researchers found that treating chronic depression normalizes abnormal brain tissue microstructure, suggesting the initial abnormalities were actually the brain’s attempt to cope with severe symptoms.

articles.title: Scientists discover why some brains resist Alzheimer's
articles.summary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
articles.aiSummary: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Some brains appear to fight back against Alzheimer's by helping immature brain cells survive damage instead of succumbing to it. Understanding this natural resilience could point researchers toward entirely new ways to protect memory and slow dementia.

articles.title: Letting go of a rigid self-image might be the secret to meditation’s mental health benefits
articles.summary: New research highlights how two distinct styles of meditation reduce symptoms of depression. While focused-attention practices and introspective self-inquiry operate through slightly different mental pathways, both help individuals detach from negative thoughts and perceived threats.
articles.aiSummary: New research highlights how two distinct styles of meditation reduce symptoms of depression. While focused-attention practices and introspective self-inquiry operate through slightly different mental pathways, both help individuals detach from negative thoughts and perceived threats.
